metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign ownership of a 'Store compatibility record' subsection in `docs/releasing.md` (covering .store-compat.json, the absent-record policy, and rollback safety) to child 21dd-7a0b-2119-4473 or one of the release stories.
findings.finding: Parent AC5 requires both `docs/releasing.md` AND `docs/migrations.md` to describe the store compatibility record (including the absent-record upgrade/rollback path). Child 21dd-7a0b-2119-4473 covers `docs/migrations.md` fully, but no child claims a section in `docs/releasing.md` for the store compatibility record. The three release stories (F1/F3/F4) each own named sections of `docs/releasing.md` ('Release-source authorization', 'Build-once', 'Pinning') but none includes store compatibility.
findings.location: child 21dd-7a0b-2119-4473 (scope/docs), child fd84-ba00-ba00-4f73 (docs/releasing.md scope), child 6168-df06-1e08-4ea3 (docs/releasing.md scope), child 08a8-b04e-ae59-4be4 (docs/releasing.md scope)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child 21dd-7a0b-2119-4473's scope: 'docs/releasing.md — add a subsection (e.g. under a "Store compatibility" heading) describing the .store-compat.json record, the absent-record upgrade path, and the rollback-safe outcome for pre-v1.0 binaries.' Extend its AC to match.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC to each child story requiring that plan-review advisory findings be recorded with Accept/Reject disposition as a ticket comment, and that every Accepted finding be incorporated into the description, before the child's Gerrit LLM-Review +1 / Verified +1 votes are cast.
findings.finding: Parent AC6 ('Each child story's plan-review advisory findings are recorded with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and a one-line rationale as a comment on the child ticket, and every Accepted finding is reflected in the child's description before that child's change receives its Gerrit votes') is not delegated to or reflected in any child story's scope or acceptance criteria. No child defines the mechanism for capturing advisory findings or enforces the disposition step.
findings.location: All five children — none covers parent AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Append to each child's Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] Plan-review advisory findings for this story are recorded with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and one-line rationale as a comment on this ticket; every Accepted finding is reflected in this description before the Gerrit LLM-Review +1 / Verified +1 votes are cast.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py` to child fd84-ba00-ba00-4f73's scope with an explicit AC that it updates any assertions in that file whose expected `release.yml` patterns change as a result of the trigger/guard re-keying.
findings.finding: Child fd84-ba00-ba00-4f73 (F1, which lands last) re-keys `release.yml` trigger and guard logic — removing `push: tags: v*`, replacing `github.ref_name`/tag-based `if:` conditions with `workflow_dispatch` conditions — but does not include `tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py` in its scope. That test file is introduced by F4 (08a8) and extended by F3 (6168) to statically assert specific `release.yml` structural patterns. F1's trigger/guard changes may remove or rename patterns the existing test assertions reference (e.g. `if: github.event_name == 'push' && startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')` guards that F1 explicitly removes), yet F1's scope and AC make no mention of updating this file.
findings.location: child fd84-ba00-ba00-4f73 (Scope section; AC bullet 2)
findings.suggested_fix: In fd84's Scope section, add: 'tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py — update any assertions whose expected release.yml patterns are invalidated by the trigger change (e.g. tag-based if: guards replaced by dispatch conditions; github.ref_name version derivation replaced by dispatch input), ensuring make test passes after F1 lands on top of F4+F3.' Add a matching AC bullet.

findings.finding: Parent AC6 ('Each child story's plan-review advisory findings are recorded with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and a one-line rationale as a comment on the child ticket, and every Accepted finding is reflected in the child's description before that child's change receives its Gerrit LLM-Review +1 / Verified +1 votes') is not encoded as a verifiable acceptance criterion in any of the five children. No child has an AC item requiring this disposition record, so the completion verifier has no per-child evidence to inspect and the gate can be bypassed silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a final AC item to each child story: '[ ] Plan-review advisory findings for this story are recorded as a comment on this ticket with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and one-line rationale; every Accepted finding is incorporated into this description before the change receives Gerrit LLM-Review +1 / Verified +1 votes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the 'MCP publisher' binary/package (package name, install source, and current pinned version+digest) in the Finding 4 success criterion so an executor can act on it without investigation.
findings.finding: The Success Criteria for Finding 4 mentions 'an exact-versioned + digest-verified MCP publisher' as one of the immutable references the release pipeline must use, but 'MCP publisher' is never identified — no package name, binary name, or registry is given. An executor cannot SHA-pin or digest-verify a tool they cannot name.
findings.location: Success Criteria, bullet 4: 'Every executable the release depends on is identified by an immutable reference (full action SHAs, a hash-locked build environment, an exact-versioned + digest-verified MCP publisher)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an exact-versioned + digest-verified MCP publisher' with the concrete tool name and current pin, e.g., 'the `modelcontextprotocol` CLI pinned to v0.x.y (SHA256: <digest>) installed via `pip install --require-hashes`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the chosen build-environment locking mechanism (e.g., 'uv.lock checked into the repo, resolved via `uv sync --frozen` in CI') in the Finding 4 / Finding 3 success criteria so the executor has a decided default to implement.
findings.finding: 'Hash-locked build environment' is specified as a requirement in the Success Criteria but the locking mechanism is not named, making this a 'choose an appropriate X' decision deferred to the executor rather than a plan-level decision.
findings.location: Success Criteria, bullet 4: 'a hash-locked build environment'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Success Criteria bullet: 'hash-locked build environment (uv.lock, resolved with `uv sync --frozen`; no network resolution during the release build step)' — or whichever tool the project already uses for dependency management.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the three-way contradiction on corrupt/unreadable record handling: add 'corrupt/unreadable → fail-closed' explicitly to the Context section's case enumeration AND add a matching verification clause to AC bullet 4 so the corrupt/unreadable path is both described and testable.
findings.finding: The Success Criteria specifies that a 'corrupt/unreadable' store compatibility record must also trigger fail-closed behavior, but the Context section only defines two cases (absent=fail-open, present-but-incompatible=fail-closed) and never mentions corrupt/unreadable. The corresponding AC verification line for Finding 2 also only checks 'present-but-incompatible' fail-closed behavior, leaving the corrupt/unreadable case uncovered by any AC and contradicting the Context framing.
findings.location: Context section (Finding 2 paragraph) vs Success Criteria bullet 2 vs AC bullet 4
findings.suggested_fix: In the Context section, extend the two-case enumeration to three: (a) absent → legacy/compatible (fail-open, ensure-registry writes on next covered write), (b) present-but-incompatible → fail-closed, (c) corrupt/unreadable → fail-closed. In AC bullet 4, add: '…and a corrupt or unreadable record also causes fail-closed (no side effects permitted).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit T4 disposition for Finding 5: state that the hard-fail on removed inputs is an intentional, justified breaking change at v1.0 (not a phased deprecation), describe the rollback path (downgrade to pre-v1.0 restores silent-ignore behavior), and note this in the child story's AC so the Gerrit reviewer can verify the choice is conscious.
findings.finding: Finding 5's fail-loud / nonzero-exit behavior for removed env vars and TOML keys is a breaking change for existing users who have deprecated inputs set in their environment or config files, but the plan specifies no rollback path, no expand-contract sequence (e.g., warn-then-fail across releases), and no explicit justification that a hard-break at v1.0 is the chosen policy rather than a phased deprecation.
findings.location: Finding 5 description and Success Criteria bullet 5
findings.suggested_fix: In the Finding 5 child story description, add a one-paragraph 'Compat decision' section: (1) justify why v1.0 is the right moment for a hard-break rather than a warn-then-fail cycle; (2) state the rollback path explicitly ('downgrading to pre-v1.0 restores the original silent-ignore behavior; the config key should simply be removed from the user's environment/file'); (3) add a docs/migrations.md entry listing each tombstoned key with the migration action.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC bullet requiring docs/migrations.md (or a dedicated Finding 5 section therein) to enumerate each tombstoned input (env var and TOML key), the migration action for each, and the project's chosen forward-compatibility policy for genuinely unknown keys.
findings.finding: The AC's documentation requirements (docs/releasing.md and docs/migrations.md updates) cover the release-authorization gate, build-once artifact flow, and store compatibility record, but do not mention any documentation obligation for Finding 5's new tombstone/hard-failure contract — a new observable CLI/config behavior that existing users and integrators need to know about.
findings.location: AC bullet: 'docs/releasing.md and docs/migrations.md are updated to describe the new release-authorization gate, the build-once artifact flow, and the store compatibility record'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the existing AC doc bullet to read: '…and the store compatibility record; docs/migrations.md also enumerates each tombstoned env var and TOML key (TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR, REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE, verify.require_verdict_for_close, etc.) with the migration action and the project's forward-compatibility policy for unknown keys.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RED fixture or probe step (in the Finding 2 child story) that exercises a pre-v1.0 store reader against a store containing the new compatibility-record file, confirming the old binary treats it as an ignored-unknown file before the change is committed.
findings.finding: The rollback-safety claim for Finding 2 — 'the record is an ignored-unknown file to old binaries' — is an unverified assumption about existing pre-v1.0 code behavior. If old binaries scan or validate all store files (e.g., treating unrecognised files as corruption), the rollback path breaks silently. The plan asserts this but does not include a spike or RED fixture to confirm it before full build-out.
findings.location: Context — Finding 2; Success Criteria paragraph 2; AC line 'rollback-safe outcome for a pre-v1.0 binary encountering the record'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Finding 2 child story, add a 'rollback probe' acceptance criterion: write the new compatibility record into a test store, then run the current (pre-patch) store-reading code path against it and assert it produces no error. This can be a unit test using the existing store reader before the gate is wired in, pinned as a RED test that turns green only if rollback is safe.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Finding 2 child story how the gate is structurally enforced across all write paths (e.g., a single store-façade entry point, a decorator/middleware, or a pre-write hook), and capture this architectural decision in an ADR or CLAUDE.md update.
findings.finding: The store compatibility gate (Finding 2) must be wired into 8+ distinct write paths (append, transition, claim, delete, archive, compaction, MCP writes, automatic repair, reconciler inbound, Jira push/reconcile), but the plan specifies no structural coupling-mitigation pattern — no single choke-point, middleware layer, or interface boundary is mentioned. Scattered per-path checks make future gate-logic changes expensive and error-prone (easy to miss a path).
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'before any side effect across all store-mutation/publish paths (append/transition/claim/delete/archive, compaction, MCP writes, automatic repair, the reconciler inbound write path, and Jira push/reconcile)'
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a single `StoreWriter` façade or `pre_write_gate()` hook that all write paths call before any side effect. Document this pattern in `docs/adr/` (or CLAUDE.md) so future write paths know where to integrate. The Finding 2 child story's AC should include: 'all covered write paths go through the gate via [named pattern] — verified by grepping for direct store writes that bypass the façade'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC bullet requiring `docs/migrations.md` to document Finding 5's tombstoned env vars and TOML keys (listing each removed input, its replacement or removal rationale, and the targeted migration message text), so callers depending on the old silent-ignore behavior have a documented upgrade path.
findings.finding: Finding 5 introduces a breaking behavioral change for existing users (previously-silent ignore of removed env vars/TOML keys becomes a hard-fail with nonzero exit), but docs/migrations.md coverage is scoped in the AC only to the store compatibility record — there is no explicit requirement for Finding 5's tombstone changes to appear in migrations.md or any migration guide, leaving callers who depend on the old silent-ignore behavior without documented guidance.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — fifth bullet (`docs/releasing.md` and `docs/migrations.md` are updated…); Finding 5 description
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '`docs/migrations.md` includes a Finding 5 section listing each tombstoned env var and TOML key (TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR, REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE, verify.require_verdict_for_close, and any others), the targeted migration message text, and the replacement or removal rationale for each.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Finding 3's child story must specify that the wheel is built directly from the git checkout (e.g. `python -m build --wheel` or equivalent, not the default two-step sdist-then-wheel path) so that `hatch_build.py` has access to `.git` and bakes a non-null COMMIT; the hash-verification test should additionally assert `COMMIT != None` in the installed wheel's `_build_info.py`.
findings.finding: Finding 3's Success Criterion ('the shipped wheel carries the correct non-null source commit') commits the plan to fixing the COMMIT=None defect, but the plan does not state the root-cause fix mechanism: `python -m build` (default) builds the wheel FROM the sdist in a tmpdir with no git history, causing hatch_build.py's `_build_commit` to return None even when run in a git checkout. Simply installing and hash-verifying the artifact before publishing catches the symptom (a test asserting non-null COMMIT), but only if the wheel BUILD step itself is changed (e.g. `python -m build --wheel` to build directly from the git tree, bypassing the sdist-untar path). Without stating this mechanism change, an implementer may build identically to today and write a test that cannot detect the flaw.
findings.location: Finding 3 / Success Criteria ('the shipped wheel carries the correct non-null source commit') / hatch_build.py
findings.suggested_fix: In the Finding 3 child story, change the build invocation from `python -m build` (which builds sdist first, then wheel from sdist in a no-git tmpdir) to `python -m build --wheel` (wheel directly from the git working tree) followed by a separate `python -m build --sdist`. The hash-verification step should also assert the installed `rebar._build_info.COMMIT` is not None.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Finding 5's child story should state the positive rationale for the nonzero-exit (vs warn-and-continue) posture for tombstoned high-impact inputs and clarify the detection boundary (e.g. at config-load for write/mutating paths only, or universally), so implementers can correctly scope the abort behavior.
findings.finding: Finding 5's Success Criterion ('removed inputs that affect store location, write/sync gates, auth, security, or lifecycle policy fail loud with a targeted migration message and a nonzero exit') specifies fail-loud behavior, but the plan does not state the positive rationale for WHY this particular detection boundary (env-var presence / TOML key presence at config-load time) and severity (nonzero exit / process abort) is preferable to the alternative: enriching the existing deprecation-registry to emit a startup warning (non-fatal) with a targeted message. Without this rationale, an implementer cannot decide whether 'nonzero exit' means at config-load startup, at first affected operation, or only at the specific operation guarded by the removed input.
findings.location: Finding 5 / Success Criteria / Context section 'removed env vars are silently ignored'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that github.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry publishes per-release checksums or a cosign/SLSA attestation for the mcp-publisher binary, and document the concrete digest-verification mechanism (e.g. `sha256sum -c`, `cosign verify-blob`) in the Finding 4 child story before committing to 'digest-verified' in the Success Criteria.
findings.finding: The Success Criteria for Finding 4 asserts 'an exact-versioned + digest-verified MCP publisher' as a required immutable reference, but it is unverified whether the MCP Registry project (github.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry) publishes per-release checksums/SBOMs alongside the mcp-publisher binary tarballs, which is the prerequisite for digest verification in practice. The current release.yml fetches `releases/latest/download/mcp-publisher_*.tar.gz` (no version, no digest). Pinning the version in the URL is straightforward, but digest verification requires the upstream to publish a SHA256 manifest — a capability that is asserted but not confirmed.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 4 ('exact-versioned + digest-verified MCP publisher'); .github/workflows/release.yml lines 127-129 (Install mcp-publisher step)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete post-rollback schema state for Finding 2: assert whether the compatibility record persists in the store tree after a pre-v1.0 rollback, name the mechanism by which old binaries ignore it (e.g., not-scanned directory location, gitignored, or old-binary path simply doesn't read it), and confirm no old-binary code path errors on an unexpected committed file.
findings.finding: The plan's rollback safety claim ('the record is an ignored-unknown file to old binaries') asserts behavioral intent but does not specify the concrete schema STATE of the store after rollback — the plan does not state whether the record persists in the committed store tree after a rollback to a pre-v1.0 binary, nor how existing pre-v1.0 binaries concretely handle an unexpected committed file in the store (silent ignore, warning, or scan-triggered error). Per T11(e), the plan must assert what the schema state IS after rollback, not merely that rollback is safe.
findings.location: Context section (Finding 2) and Success Criteria bullet: 'a rollback to a pre-v1.0 binary leaves the store operable (the record is an ignored-unknown file to old binaries)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Finding 2 description: 'Post-rollback state: the record file remains in the committed store branch; pre-v1.0 binaries do not scan for or read this file path (cite the specific code path that confirms this), so the store remains fully operable. If pre-v1.0 code does scan the directory, the record must be git-ignored or placed in a path the old binary explicitly tolerates.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Finding 4 child story how the mcp-publisher binary will be both version-pinned AND digest-verified (e.g., download to a temp file then `sha256sum --check` against a committed `.sha256` file, or use `cosign verify-blob`), since `curl | tar` cannot verify a digest mid-stream.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'an exact-versioned + digest-verified MCP publisher' (Finding 4 success criterion), but the current fetch mechanism is `curl -L …/releases/latest/download/… | tar xz mcp-publisher` — a streaming pipe that cannot natively verify a digest mid-flight. Achieving digest verification requires a two-step pattern (download to file, then sha256sum --check against a committed hash), which is a new CI step pattern not currently present in release.yml. The plan does not specify this mechanism, leaving the pinning surface UNVERIFIED at the epic level.
findings.location: Success Criteria, bullet 4: 'an exact-versioned + digest-verified MCP publisher'; .github/workflows/release.yml:127-129 (Install mcp-publisher step)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Finding 4 child story, replace the current curl-pipe step with: (1) `curl -L …/v<EXACT_VERSION>/… -o mcp-publisher.tar.gz`, (2) `echo '<SHA256>  mcp-publisher.tar.gz' | sha256sum --check`, (3) `tar xz -f mcp-publisher.tar.gz mcp-publisher`. Commit the pinned version and expected SHA256 in a file (e.g., `.github/mcp-publisher.sha256`) so Dependabot or a manual bump process can track it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Finding 4's child story should specify that the mcp-publisher binary digest is verified *before* any step that exercises the OIDC token (i.e., before `mcp-publisher login github-oidc`), or alternatively that the download + verify + execute are structured so an integrity failure aborts the job before the OIDC grant is used — the ordering of 'digest-verified' relative to OIDC consumption must be explicit.
findings.finding: The plan commits to digest-verifying the mcp-publisher binary (Finding 4 Success Criteria: 'exact-versioned + digest-verified MCP publisher') but does not specify that this verification occurs *before* the binary is executed in a step where the OIDC id-token is available. In the current workflow shape, `./mcp-publisher login github-oidc` runs immediately after download — a malicious substituted binary would receive the live OIDC token before any digest check can gate it. The plan's OIDC-scoped posture for the mcp_registry job is otherwise sound, but the verification ordering relative to OIDC token consumption is undeclared.
findings.location: .github/workflows/release.yml mcp_registry job (lines 127-134); plan Success Criteria: 'an exact-versioned + digest-verified MCP publisher'
findings.suggested_fix: Structure the mcp_registry job so the install step downloads a pinned version URL, computes the SHA-256 of the extracted binary, and compares it against a committed expected digest — all before the `mcp-publisher login github-oidc` step. Example: `curl -L .../releases/download/v1.2.3/mcp-publisher_linux_amd64.tar.gz | tar xz mcp-publisher && echo '<expected-sha256>  mcp-publisher' | sha256sum -c` as a single atomic step, then `./mcp-publisher login github-oidc` as the next step — ensuring a digest mismatch exits nonzero before the OIDC token is ever passed to the binary.

findings.finding: Finding 2's plan states 'the idempotent ensure-registry writes the record on the next covered write' and that 'upgrade-in-place works with no manual step', but the existing architecture explicitly decouples the write-hot-path from ensure sweeps: `write_and_push` calls only `maybe_emit_pending_hint` (a nudge, never a repair), while `run_ensures` (which actually writes ensure units) fires only at init/re-init, `fsck --repair`, and MCP server boot. An implementer following the plan literally would either incorrectly wire `run_ensures` into the hot write path (violating the explicit hot-path budget design in `src/rebar/_store/ensures.py` and `docs/migrations.md`) or would silently leave the format-version record unwritten for CLI-only users who never hit one of those three entry points.
findings.location: Finding 2 / Context section ('the idempotent ensure-registry writes the record on the next covered write') and AC bullet ('record auto-written by the ensure-registry on next covered write')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the idempotent ensure-registry writes the record on the next covered write' with 'the idempotent ensure-registry writes the record at the existing `run_ensures` entry points (init/re-init, `rebar fsck --repair`, and MCP server boot)' — and update the AC bullet to match. This stays true to the existing hot-path budget design, is still 'no manual step' for the two most common upgrade paths (re-init or MCP restart), and avoids over-promising for CLI-only users.

findings.finding: Child 21dd-7a0b-2119-4473's AC7 enumerates what docs/migrations.md will document ('the compatibility record and the fail-closed gate, including the deployment-order rationale') but does not explicitly commit to documenting the rollback-safe outcome for a pre-v1.0 binary encountering the record. Parent AC5 requires docs/migrations.md to include 'the absent-record upgrade/rollback path,' and parent AC4 requires the Finding 2 story to document 'a rollback-safe outcome for a pre-v1.0 binary encountering the record.' The test/analysis obligation is in 21dd AC4, but the documentation location is not explicitly bound to docs/migrations.md.
findings.location: child 21dd-7a0b-2119-4473, Acceptance Criteria item 7 (docs/migrations.md) vs parent AC4 and AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Amend 21dd AC7 to read: 'docs/migrations.md documents the compatibility record, the fail-closed gate, the absent-record legacy/compatible policy (upgrade-in-place with no manual step), the rollback-safe outcome for a pre-v1.0 binary encountering the record, and the deployment-order rationale (why the reader ships in v1.0).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
